findings.category: A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures
findings.title: cause.message propagated verbatim — may leak sensitive data (HF tokens, auth headers, URLs with query params)
findings.description: `buildLine` writes `cause.message` (or `cause::class.simpleName` fallback) directly into the on-device log after only whitespace sanitization + 500-char truncate. Exceptions thrown by OkHttp/Retrofit/HttpURLConnection frequently embed the full request URL (including query string) and sometimes header fragments into `IOException.message` / `HttpException.message`. patterns.md line 19 explicitly lists 'HuggingFace auth failed' as a target component: if the downloader ever throws `HttpException(401, 'https://huggingface.co/...?download=true&hf_token=hf_XXXXXXX')` (some client libs put the URL in the message), the token lands on disk in plaintext. Even without tokens, file-system paths under `/data/user/0/app.sanctum.machina/files/models/...` leak the package layout which, combined with any other side-channel, helps an attacker with adb debug access. Log file is local-only per patterns.md, but Android backup (auto-backup is on by default unless `android:allowBackup="false"` is set in `:app` manifest) would exfiltrate `filesDir/logs/errors.log` to Google Drive.
findings.impact: Bearer tokens, auth headers, or URL query secrets captured in exception messages are persisted in `filesDir/logs/errors.log`. If `android:allowBackup="true"` (the default), Google auto-backup uploads the log to the user's Drive. Even without backup, any future feature that reads the log for diagnostics would expose the secret.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) redact known secret patterns before writing — e.g. `causeMsg.replace(Regex("hf_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}"), "hf_***").replace(Regex("(?i)(authorization|bearer|token|key)=\\S+"), "$1=***")` — or (b) write only `cause::class.qualifiedName` and a caller-provided, already-sanitized description, never raw `cause.message`. Option (b) is cleaner but requires every call site to build the message. Minimum fix: ensure `:app/src/main/AndroidManifest.xml` sets `android:allowBackup="false"` (or a `BackupAgent` that excludes `files/logs/`) before shipping Phase 1 + add the redaction regex. Revisit in Task 6 when real call sites exist.

findings.title: `component` is written unsanitized — a malformed caller can forge log entries
findings.description: `buildLine` interpolates `component` directly into the output line without calling `sanitize()` (lines 70-73). The KDoc (lines 30-32) and tasks/5.md (line 24) explicitly document this as an accepted-risk design: the whitelist `download|inference-init|inference|inference-cleanup` is enforced only by code-reviewer on downstream callers. However, the failure mode is not just a misspelled component — any future call site that accidentally concatenates attacker-controllable data into the `component` parameter (e.g. `errorLog.e("download-" + modelId, ...)` where `modelId` comes from an allowlist entry or, worse, a user-specified URL in a Phase 2+ feature) could inject `]\nERROR [download` into the log stream and forge arbitrary 'ERROR [...] ...' lines. Task 4 (AllowlistLoader) already loads model IDs from a bundled JSON, and user-spec D11 suggests future phases may accept user-typed model identifiers.
findings.impact: Log-forgery of fake error events, which could mislead QA in Task 14 (manual `adb shell cat files/logs/errors.log` inspection) or a future log-shipping feature. Internal-only threat in Phase 1 (all callers are trusted code paths added in Task 6), but the protection is a convention, not a code invariant — one accidental string concat in a future phase breaks it silently.
findings.recommendation: Apply `sanitize()` to `component` too (it is cheap and idempotent for whitelist values that contain only `[a-z-]`): `val head = "ERROR [${sanitize(component)}] ${sanitize(description)}"`. Defence-in-depth with zero semantic cost for legitimate callers. Alternatively, enforce the whitelist in code with a `require(component in ALLOWED_COMPONENTS)` — trades flexibility for strictness, but aligns the KDoc claim with runtime reality.

findings.title: `sanitize()` regex misses Unicode line separators, vertical tab, form feed, and NUL
findings.description: `CONTROL_WS = Regex("[\\n\\r\\t]")` covers only LF, CR, HTAB. It does NOT match: vertical tab `\u000B`, form feed `\u000C`, NEL `\u0085`, LINE SEPARATOR `\u2028`, PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR `\u2029`, or NUL `\u0000`. If an exception message contains one of these (e.g. a filename with `\u2028` from a crafted model archive, or a NUL-byte from a native-code C-string truncation), the sanitized line could still contain a logical line break when viewed in editors that honour Unicode line terminators (Android Studio log viewer, `grep -P`, most Java `BufferedReader.readLine` implementations treat LF/CR only — safe — but `java.util.Scanner` and some shells like PowerShell split on `\u2028`). NUL could truncate the line in C-string based viewers.
findings.impact: Low in Phase 1 (QA uses `adb shell cat`, which is LF-only). Becomes real if a later phase ships the log to a web dashboard, parses it with `Scanner`, or displays it in a Compose `Text` that respects `\u2028`.
findings.recommendation: Broaden the regex: `private val CONTROL_WS = Regex("[\\p{Cntrl}\\u2028\\u2029]")` — `\p{Cntrl}` covers LF/CR/HTAB/VT/FF/NEL/NUL and all other C0/C1 controls, plus explicit LINE/PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR.

findings.title: `runCatching { }` swallows `CancellationException`, breaking coroutine cancellation contract
findings.description: `runCatching` catches `Throwable`, which includes `kotlinx.coroutines.CancellationException`. If the caller coroutine is cancelled while `appendText` or `renameTo` is in flight (e.g. the download job is cancelled by the user), the cancellation is swallowed and the coroutine continues past the `e()` call instead of propagating cancellation. Over many calls this can keep cancelled work alive briefly, mask DoS scenarios where a runaway loop of failed downloads spams the log, and violates the structured-concurrency contract.
findings.impact: Primarily correctness; security-adjacent because it makes shutdown / cancellation-based rate-limiting unreliable. Internal logger, so blast radius is small.
findings.recommendation: Replace `runCatching { ... }` with an explicit try/catch that re-throws cancellation: `try { ... } catch (e: CancellationException) { throw e } catch (_: Throwable) { /* swallow */ }`. Kotlin coroutines style guide and `kotlin.runCatching` KDoc both call this out explicitly.

findings.title: `component` parameter is unbounded — a 10 MB string bypasses the per-field cap
findings.description: `MAX_FIELD_LEN` caps `description` and `cause.message` at 500 chars, giving a hard per-line upper bound ~1050 bytes and, combined with 2 MB rotation, ~4 MB total on-disk footprint. `component` is NOT truncated (see finding above on sanitization). A buggy or malicious caller passing a multi-MB `component` writes one giant line, triggers rotation, and leaves that giant line sitting in `errors.log.1` — bypassing the 2 MB intent for the rotated copy.
findings.impact: Disk-space DoS via oversized `component`. Practical only with a compromised or severely buggy caller — Phase 1 callers are hard-coded strings. Rotation still works per-file, so the cap isn't broken catastrophically, just weakened.
findings.recommendation: Apply `sanitize()` to `component` (which includes the `.take(MAX_FIELD_LEN)` truncate) per the log-injection finding above — this fixes both issues in one line.

findings.title: `renameTo` silent failure leaves `errors.log` growing past 2 MB indefinitely
findings.description: `file.renameTo(rotated)` on line 62 returns a `Boolean` that is discarded. On Android, `renameTo` can fail (return `false`) for several reasons: filesystem permission quirks, a concurrent file handle held open by a log-tail tool, cross-mount renames, or SELinux policy glitches. The code never checks the return, so if rotation silently fails once, `errors.log` keeps growing by ~1 KB per error event, past 2 MB, past 10 MB, bounded only by device free space. Every subsequent `e()` call re-tries the rename and discards the result.
findings.impact: Disk-space DoS if rotation is persistently broken. Realistic trigger: a test tool or Android Studio Device Explorer holding `errors.log` open for read. Low probability, low blast radius.
findings.recommendation: Either assert the rename (`check(file.renameTo(rotated)) { "log rotation failed" }` — but that would throw and the current design swallows exceptions anyway) or fall back to `file.copyTo(rotated, overwrite = true); file.writeText("")` on `renameTo` returning false. Given the `runCatching` envelope, the cleanest fix is: `if (!file.renameTo(rotated)) { file.copyTo(rotated, overwrite = true); file.writeText("") }`.
